lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market contract, toll, private-label, and white-label manufacturers providing repeat formulation, blending, filling, packaging, labeling, and fulfillment of soaps and detergents for external brands, retailers, chemical companies, and institutional customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Manufacturers primarily selling proprietary brands, captive plants, distributors without manufacturing, handcrafted microbusinesses below the economic band, and producers focused on shampoos, cosmetics, specialty cleaners, or sanitation preparations outside NAICS 325611.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Retailers, consumer brands, industrial and institutional suppliers, and chemical companies purchase recurring finished-product runs or toll conversion under specifications and purchase orders; economics range from customer-owned formula and materials to turnkey formulation, packaging, and logistics.
lens.deviation_from_default: The NAICS combines branded product manufacturers with outsourced contract and private-label plants. The lens is narrowed to the latter because only they supply a coherent recurring outsourced manufacturing service to external customers.

a.bridge: NIST documents AI opportunities in small manufacturing for predictive maintenance, quality, scrap, throughput, and forecasting, while contract soap manufacturers disclose formulation, lab, blending, packaging, logistics, and customer-service activities. I translate these broader workflows into a wage-weighted estimate: planning, demand forecasting, formula and document retrieval, label review, scheduling, quoting, customer support, and quality trend analysis are exposed, while charging, mixing, filling, changeover, sanitation, testing, material handling, and maintenance remain physical. Cross-industry success cases likely overstate readiness in smaller, low-margin plants.
a.rationale: Contract soap and detergent operations include meaningful information work but are dominated by physical batch production, packaging, warehousing, and quality execution. Exposure is material yet clearly below a majority of wages.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task census and time study across operators, compounders, fillers, QC, formulators, planners, procurement, customer service, sales, and administration at five or more LMM contract plants would materially move the range.
caveats: No source measures AI-exposed tasks for NAICS 325611 or the narrowed contract-manufacturing subset.
Existing packaging and process automation varies materially by product form and run length.

rho.bridge: NIST's small-manufacturer cases show feasible AI benefits but stress defined problems, usable historical data, leadership, and incremental pilots. I apply those constraints to soap and detergent contract plants, with faster implementation in office, labeling, planning, and forecasting workflows and slower implementation in closed-loop process control or formula changes. The source's selected cases may overstate representative implementation, while lower regulatory intensity than pharmaceuticals supports a somewhat higher five-year rate.
rho.rationale: Standardized high-volume workflows and abundant ERP, order, and QC data can support adoption. Fragmented systems, confidentiality, retailer specifications, environmental compliance, packaging complexity, and limited technical staff constrain the exposed share implemented.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after deployment data from LMM household-product contract manufacturers showing integration cost, sustained utilization, prediction error, changeover or scrap reduction, and labor hours avoided would materially move the estimate.
caveats: Selected AI projects do not establish an industry adoption rate.
Implementation excludes pricing, market demand, and valuation effects.

e.rationale: Multiple US manufacturers explicitly provide contract blending, private label, formula development, packaging, and fulfillment, so outsourced production is a meaningful industry model. Eligibility remains below half because major branded manufacturers, vertically integrated producers, and mixed-model firms populate the NAICS, while some contract firms fall outside the LMM band.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm classification of the provided LMM population using D&B or PitchBook, websites, customer and brand disclosures, and revenue splits between proprietary and external contract production would materially change eligibility.
caveats: The provided firm count is based on a specialty-chemical margin bridge rather than observed soap-manufacturer EBITDA.
Private-label soap may coexist with cosmetics or specialty-cleaner activities classified elsewhere.

s5.bridge: Recent disclosed US transactions include a lower-middle-market acquisition of contract cleaning-products packager Ultra-Pak and a majority investment in contract cleaning-products formulator Formula Corp. These establish active buyer interest but do not provide a denominator or five-year probability for all eligible soap and detergent firms. I infer a moderately active transfer market, discounting for environmental liabilities, customer concentration, and nonqualifying asset or minority deals. Public announcements undercount private transfers but selected deals overrepresent successful outcomes.
s5.rationale: Private equity and strategic buyers are demonstrably acquiring contract and private-label cleaning-product manufacturers, and founder-owned plants offer succession opportunities. Some firms remain family-held, too small, operationally dependent, or encumbered by plant liabilities.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year PitchBook and GF Data census of US contract household-cleaning manufacturer control deals, divided by an eligible-firm denominator and supplemented with owner-age interviews, would materially move the estimate.
caveats: Two announced deals demonstrate flow but cannot measure probability.
Some target activities may include personal care or specialty cleaners outside strict NAICS 325611.

q.bridge: Church & Dwight's consumer-products filing shows that manufacturing and commodity cost increases can outweigh productivity and that pricing, retailer inventories, product volume, and private-label competition affect results. Toll providers disclose customer-owned formulas and facility-and-labor conversion models. I translate these large-brand and toll-model observations to LMM contract manufacturers: turnkey formulation and reliable service support retention, while powerful retailers, visible conversion costs, competitive bids, and customer-owned intellectual property expose savings. Brand-company economics likely overstate LMM pricing power.
q.rationale: Contract manufacturing is commonly priced per unit, batch, or conversion charge and is regularly rebid. Savings tied to labor alone are vulnerable to repricing; savings that improve speed, fill rate, regulatory execution, scrap, or minimum runs are more retainable.
q.change_evidence: Customer-contract and renewal data showing open-book costs, raw-material pass-through, productivity sharing, rebates, service-level penalties, formula ownership, and post-improvement gross margins would materially move retention.
caveats: Retailer private-label, institutional, and customer-supplied toll contracts have different bargaining structures.
This estimate excludes implementation difficulty and demand changes.

d5.bridge: A nationally representative 2025 ACI survey found 97% of US adults viewed cleaning and hygiene as important for public health and 74% had changed cleaning habits to improve health, supporting a durable consumption base. Church & Dwight reported mixed 2025 household-product volumes, showing near-term category and brand volatility. I translate consumer attitudes and a branded producer's results into modest five-year constant-price quantity growth for outsourced soap and detergent manufacturing. Survey attitudes may overstate actual product volume, while branded results may understate private-label outsourcing.
d5.rationale: Laundry, dishwashing, handwashing, and routine cleaning are recurring necessities, with population and private-label expansion supporting modest growth. Product concentration, dose reduction, refill formats, customer insourcing, and retailer inventory cycles constrain quantity growth.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year US unit or tonnage forecasts for laundry, dish, and soap categories combined with retailer private-label share, contract-manufacturing outsourcing share, and target backlog would materially move the range.
caveats: The ACI survey measures attitudes and habits rather than soap or detergent volume.
Church & Dwight is a branded diversified company, not an LMM contract manufacturer.

o.rationale: Year-five demand still requires physical formulation, chemical handling, blending or soap processing, equipment cleanout, wastewater controls, filling, packaging, and shipment. Software may automate decisions and ordering but cannot deliver the current physical service basket without an accountable manufacturer.
o.change_evidence: Verified widespread adoption of autonomous in-store or customer-site detergent microfactories that replaces external contract volumes, rather than merely changing packaging, would materially lower the estimate.
caveats: Concentrates, refill systems, and dosing innovation can reduce shipped quantity without removing the manufacturing operator.
The estimate concerns operator requirement, not unchanged staffing.

narrative.executive_view: The relevant pool is the subset of soap and detergent plants providing external contract, private-label, or toll production. Essential repeat consumption and active buyer interest support the model, but procurement power and visible conversion economics can limit retention of AI-driven labor savings.
narrative.ai_impact: Practical uses include formula and specification retrieval, label and document review, demand and inventory forecasting, production scheduling, quote drafting, customer-service automation, predictive quality, changeover planning, and maintenance triage. Mixing, filling, sanitation, QC sampling, wastewater control, warehousing, and shipping remain physical.
narrative.value_capture: Retailer and brand customers often have alternatives and can rebid per-unit economics. The operator retains more value when AI improves fill rate, rapid minimum runs, compliance, formulation speed, scrap, and on-time delivery than when it merely reveals fewer direct labor hours.
narrative.firm_availability: Public transaction evidence shows that contract and private-label cleaning-product manufacturers are being acquired by lower-middle-market sponsors and strategic buyers. The addressable population still excludes branded manufacturers and depends on clean environmental diligence, transferable formulas, customer diversity, and stable plant leadership.
narrative.demand_durability: Soap and detergent usage is tied to routine household, institutional, and industrial hygiene. Quantity should remain stable to modestly growing, though concentrated formulations, packaging changes, retailer inventory decisions, and brand shifts can move individual contract-plant volumes.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Eligibility and commercial retention lack direct measurement. Retailer concentration, private-label price competition, raw-material and packaging volatility, environmental liabilities, product quality, formula confidentiality, working capital, customer insourcing, and legacy plant systems can overwhelm the labor opportunity.
narrative.principal_driver: Essential repeat cleaning demand and outsourced private-label production create durable physical throughput with recurring AI-addressable planning and quality work.
narrative.principal_weakness: Powerful customers can reprice transparent conversion savings, leaving the manufacturer with implementation and plant risk but only part of the benefit.

sources.title: Artificial Intelligence in Manufacturing: Real World Success Stories and Lessons Learned
sources.supports: NIST MEP describes AI uses and adoption requirements for small and medium manufacturers, including quality, maintenance, throughput, scrap, and forecasting.

sources.title: Southern California Soap Company Services
sources.supports: Documents contract blending and packaging, private label, custom formulation, laboratory work, broad packaging formats, and 200,000-pound daily capacity.

sources.title: Sanco Industries Toll Blending
sources.supports: Describes customer-owned formula toll blending using facility and labor, private label, packaging, repackaging, and confidentiality controls.

sources.title: Nature Plus Toll Manufacturing
sources.supports: Confirms a mixed branded, private-label, toll, and contract model covering concept, formulation, production, filling, packaging, distribution, and short or medium runs.

sources.title: Makai Capital-backed Paladin Holdings Acquires Ultra-Pak
sources.supports: Documents a 2024 lower-middle-market acquisition of a regional contract manufacturer and packager of cleaning products serving jan-san and hospitality customers.

sources.title: San Francisco Equity Partners Acquires Formula Corp
sources.supports: Documents a 2025 majority investment in a US cleaning-products contract formulator providing R&D, blending, filling, packaging, regulatory, and fulfillment services.

sources.title: Church & Dwight First Quarter 2025 Form 10-Q
sources.supports: Reports household-product volume and pricing changes, manufacturing and commodity costs, productivity benefits, retailer inventory behavior, and private-label competition.

sources.title: ACI Survey: Three-Quarters of Americans Are Changing Their Cleaning Habits
sources.supports: A 2025 nationally representative survey reports 97% consider cleaning and hygiene important for public health and 74% changed cleaning habits to improve health.

sources.title: Soap and Detergent Manufacturing Effluent Guidelines
sources.supports: EPA describes regulated NAICS 325611 production processes, wastewater sources, direct and indirect discharge controls, and 19 manufacturing subcategories.
